examples.title: FHSS Algorithm (Bluetooth)
examples.description: Demonstrates frequency hopping spread spectrum used in Bluetooth communication
examples.code:
// Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum Algorithm
// Bluetooth uses 79 channels in 2.4 GHz band
BEGIN
 // Initialize
 channelMap = [0, 1, 2, ..., 78] // 79 channels
 currentChannel = 0
 hopPattern = [0, 34, 67, 13, ...] // Pseudo-random sequence
 
 FUNCTION TransmitData(data, numHops)
 FOR i = 0 TO numHops - 1 DO
 // Calculate next frequency channel
 currentChannel = hopPattern[i % length(hopPattern)]
 frequency = 2402 + (currentChannel * 1) // 2.402 GHz + channel
 
 // Transmit data on current frequency
 TransmitOnFrequency(frequency, data)
 
 // Wait for hop time (625 microseconds)
 Wait(625 microseconds)
 END FOR
 END FUNCTION
 
 // Receiver synchronization
 FUNCTION ReceiveData(expectedPattern)
 WHILE dataAvailable DO
 // Synchronize with transmitter's hop pattern
 currentChannel = SyncWithTransmitter(expectedPattern)
 frequency = 2402 + currentChannel
 data = ReceiveOnFrequency(frequency)
 END WHILE
 END FUNCTION
END
examples.explanation: Bluetooth uses FHSS to rapidly switch between 79 channels in the 2.4 GHz ISM band. Each channel is 1 MHz wide, and the hop sequence changes every 625 microseconds. This provides resistance to interference and security.

examples.title: Simple Wireless Signal Propagation Model
examples.description: Calculates line-of-sight distance based on antenna heights
examples.explanation: This algorithm calculates the maximum line-of-sight distance between two antennas based on Earth's radius and antenna heights. For practical communication, antennas must be high enough to clear obstacles and the Fresnel zone.

examples.title: Wi-Fi Channel Selection Algorithm
examples.description: Demonstrates how Wi-Fi access points select channels to minimize interference
examples.code:
// Simple Wi-Fi Channel Selection Algorithm
// For 2.4 GHz band (channels 1-14, 5 MHz spacing)

CONSTANTS
 NUM_CHANNELS = 14
 CHANNEL_WIDTH = 20 // MHz
 
FUNCTION ScanChannels()
 // Scan all channels and measure interference
 channelScanResults = []
 
 FOR channel = 1 TO NUM_CHANNELS DO
 noiseLevel = MeasureNoise(channel)
 interference = MeasureInterference(channel)
 channelScanResults[channel] = noiseLevel + interference
 END FOR
 
 RETURN channelScanResults
END FUNCTION

FUNCTION SelectBestChannel(scanResults)
 // For 2.4 GHz, use non-overlapping channels: 1, 6, 11
 nonOverlappingChannels = [1, 6, 11]
 
 bestChannel = -1
 minInterference = INFINITY
 
 FOR each ch IN nonOverlappingChannels DO
 IF scanResults[ch] < minInterference THEN
 minInterference = scanResults[ch]
 bestChannel = ch
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 RETURN bestChannel
END FUNCTION

// Main execution
scanResults = ScanChannels()
selectedChannel = SelectBestChannel(scanResults)
PRINT "Best channel: " + selectedChannel
examples.explanation: This algorithm demonstrates how Wi-Fi access points automatically select the least congested channel. In the 2.4 GHz band, channels 1, 6, and 11 are non-overlapping (20 MHz spacing). The algorithm scans all channels, measures interference, and selects the optimal channel.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n = number of channels
